moveAnnotations.annotation: We're playing the Hungarian Opening, an unorthodox system where we prepare to fianchetto our kingside bishop on g2, controlling the long diagonal rather than claiming the center immediately. This move is subtly flexible—we avoid the theoretical battles of 1.e4 or 1.d4, instead building a solid position where our bishop on g2 will exert quiet pressure on the c1-h6 long diagonal. The Hungarian has a reputation as a patient, positional setup favored by players who prefer maneuvering over forcing tactics, and it's especially useful if you study your opponent's center-dominating openings beforehand.

Black develops naturally, attacking no pawns but preparing to control the center while keeping options open—this is the flexible Indian Defense approach. By playing Nf6, Black develops a piece with tempo potential and can later decide whether to contest the center with ...e6 or ...d6, or even strike at it with moves like ...e5. This move also prevents us from playing an immediate e4, showing why the Hungarian requires patience; Black refuses to be provoked into a classical center battle and instead builds flexibility into their setup.
